insight: Tarsiers are most valuable when their build time denies absorb; when the buildtime is not denying absorb, Tarsiers are 'really meh' and other attackers should be preferred.
author: cogito3
context: Game review discussion where cachotognax used Tarsiers against a Hannibull rush, debating whether they were the right choice.

insight: Bite has good synergy with Iso Kronus in burst strategies, as the bite ability complements Iso Kronus's damage output.
author: cogito3
context: Post-game analysis discussing whether Tarsiers or Iso Kronus was better against a Hannibull rush.

insight: Fibroid is a good soak unit primarily because it threatens for 2 damage, forcing the opponent to respect the possibility of absorb and potentially requiring them to build 1 extra defense to gambit for exact damage. It also saves a drone compared to Forcefield.
author: cogito3
context: Discussion about whether Fibroid or Forcefield is better for soaking, in a set with Hannibull.

insight: Shredder should generally only be bought after the opponent has enough damage that it cannot be abused (roughly 4 plus your absorb amount), and you almost never want to click it offensively.
author: medar
context: Game review where the player bought a Shredder too early and it reduced Centurion absorb value.

insight: Doomed Drones are extremely gold-efficient defensive units; when you are certain they will get full defensive value, they should be substituted for regular Drones. In 3-engineer games, P2 should open DE1 as it is extremely gold efficient.
author: medar
context: Game review where the player built 19 drones but no Doomed Drones despite heavy defensive pressure.

insight: Buying a Wall preemptively before you immediately need it is typically incorrect; only buy a Wall when you need it right away.
author: medar
context: Game review noting the player over-defended on some turns by buying Walls prematurely.

insight: Centurion should not be rushed; there is no need to get it until the opponent's damage approaches 5 (the absorb threshold). Building Galvani Drones and a Shredder before establishing Centurion causes it to lose significant absorb value.
author: medar
context: Game review analyzing an inefficient Centurion build-up that was undermined by other purchases.

insight: Grimbotch (Botch) is almost always preferable to Tarsier when both are available; players should buy Grimbotches instead of Tarsiers if Grimbotch is in the set.
author: medar
context: Post-game analysis noting the player had too much good defense and should have prioritized Grimbotch over Tarsiers.

insight: When green is weak or absent in the unit pool and a third Engineer is not planned, P2's standard response is DD/DA ('Fastimus'), cutting a drone for an early Animus. This line gets out attackers quickly and is generally agreed to give P2 the upper hand in base-set-only games.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about P2 options when green is weak and third Engineer is not desired.

insight: Wild Drone and Doomed Drone allow P2 to open with a Conduit on turn 1 (e.g., '1, 1C with Wild Drone'), enabling early green resource generation that would otherwise require floating gold.
author: supergreyman
context: Replay review discussing how to open efficiently in a set with Wild Drone and Tantalum Ray.

insight: Tantalum Ray is typically the most efficient attacker when green is available; in sets where it is present, going Animus for Tarsiers can be strictly inferior and a 2nd Conduit may be needed to pump out Tantalum Rays each turn.
author: supergreyman
context: Replay review where the player went Tarsiers instead of Tantalum Rays and fell a turn behind.

insight: Lancetooth is only worth building when its 2-buildtime denies absorb; otherwise it functions as a worse Tarsier version of Steelsplitter. Its 2-buildtime does allow chaining two on consecutive turns if you lacked extra attack, unlike prompt attackers such as Bloodrager.
author: willflame
context: Discussion about when Lancetooth is worth building compared to other attackers.

insight: Bloodrager loses value when it no longer denies absorb; continuing to buy Bloodragers once the opponent is overdefended is usually incorrect.
author: medar
context: Game review where the player kept buying Bloodragers past the point they were denying absorb.

insight: Thunderhead provides good value when it can both attack and absorb over several turns. A single attack-then-soak Thunderhead is roughly equivalent in resource value to three Infusion Grids plus four Gauss Charges, making it worthwhile if the opponent cannot deny absorb. Its main cost is the heavy green requirement.
author: cogito3
context: Mathematical analysis of Thunderhead's value in a shredder/Thunderhead strategy discussion.

insight: Clicking Ebb Turbine to buy a Drone is almost never correct because it simply removes one Drone from supply and accomplishes nothing else. The rare exception is 'golden armor'—a unit under construction cannot be attacked, so clicking Ebb to buy a Drone can protect that Drone from being breached.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about edge cases for Ebb Turbine usage.

insight: Units under construction have 'golden armor' and cannot be targeted or attacked by the opponent. This edge case can occasionally make otherwise suboptimal purchases (like clicking Ebb to buy a Drone) correct when you need to protect a specific unit from being breached.
author: amalloy
context: Explanation of the golden armor mechanic in the context of Ebb Turbine edge cases.

insight: Shadowfang benefits greatly from triple Animus, allowing a player to produce two Shadowfangs per turn, generating overwhelming attack pressure. Shadowfang typically requires a single Flame Animus plus an Animus, while Vai Mauronax is usually supported by two Flame Animuses and one Animus.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about quad-red unit sets and how fast they make the game.

insight: Holding attack to achieve precise lethal or to kill a specific high-value unit is often better than attacking with partial force. In one example, holding a Rhino for one turn to accumulate 6 attack to precisely kill an Iceblade was correct, rather than attacking immediately for 1 damage.
author: willflame
context: Game review noting that attacking with 1 Rhino for 1 damage was worse than holding for precise Iceblade kill.

insight: Rhino-rushing to get over an absorb barrier is usually not worth it when the opponent has not yet built any damage; the damage that goes over absorb first does not matter much if the opponent can absorb the rest without buying extra defense.
author: medar
context: Game review discussing whether Rhino-rushing to break absorb on turn 4 against 0 damage was correct.

insight: As P2, buying a third Engineer can be enabled on turn 1 by purchasing one regular Drone and one Doomed Drone in sets with high-health blockers and Doomed Drones available, as detailed in 307th's blog post on alternate drone openings.
author: cogito3
context: Question about whether third-Engineer openings work with Doomed Drone in sets with high-health blockers.

insight: Tyranno Smorcus works as a Chrono Filter stabilizer, allowing a player to alternate spending RR and RB on units to smooth out the red resource curve.
author: .bky_1556
context: Player realizing mid-game that Tyranno Smorcus could have stabilized their Chrono Filter economy.

insight: An older version of Odin cost 21BBB with 5 health, and before that 18BBB with 5 health, compared to its later 16-gold version. Even the stronger 21BBB version was considered P1-favored.
author: crash_overlord
context: Historical discussion about how Odin was balanced in earlier versions of the game.

insight: Deadeye Operative sets tend to favor a pure defense-heavy strategy with no attackers, good soak, and good absorb, making units like Ebb Turbine for breachproof defense potentially correct even in large numbers (e.g., 10 Ebb Turbines).
author: 1m2o
context: Discussion about breachproof strategies in sets with Deadeye Operative and Apollo.
